metadata.description: A systems programming language focused on memory safety, zero-cost abstractions, and high performance.
pros: Memory safety without a garbage collector

metadata.description: A modern, object-oriented programming language developed by Microsoft, designed for building applications on the .NET platform with strong tooling and productivity.
pros: Good balance between performance and developer productivity
cons: Less low-level control compared to C/C++

metadata.description: Low-level programming language that provides direct control over hardware resources.
A high-performance systems programming language that combines low-level memory control with high-level abstractions such as object-oriented and generic programming.
pros: Very high performance and low-level control
Supports OOP, templates, and generic programming
Wide ecosystem (STL, game engines, embedded systems)
cons: Complex syntax and steep learning curve
Manual memory management can cause bugs

metadata.description: A high-level, general-purpose language used in backend, automation, data processing, and AI/ML, known for simplicity, readability, and rapid development.
pros: Rich ecosystem for AI, ML, data science, and automation
cons: Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) limits true parallel concurrency in CPython
Slower execution compared to compiled languages like Rust or C++
Runtime errors due to dynamic typing in large-scale systems

metadata.description: Frontend stack for building UI of web and software apps using HTML, CSS, JavaScript and frameworks like React, Vue, Angular.
pros: Rich ecosystem of frameworks (React, Vue, Angular, etc.)
Highly flexible for building complex UI/UX
Runs on all modern browsers and platforms
Supports component-based architecture for scalable apps
cons: Requires careful state management and architecture design

metadata.description: A system for observing and analyzing application and system behavior through collected data such as logs, metrics, and traces.
pros: Provides visibility into system health and performance
cons: Can generate large amounts of data

metadata.description: A technique for recording events, messages, and system activities to provide insight into application behavior and errors.
cons: Can become noisy and hard to manage

metadata.description: A technique for collecting numerical data representing system performance and behavior over time, such as latency, throughput, and resource usage.
pros: Efficient for monitoring trends over time
cons: Not suitable for debugging specific issues

metadata.description: A technique for tracking the flow of requests and operations across multiple components to understand system behavior and latency.
pros: Provides end-to-end visibility of request flow
Helps identify bottlenecks and latency issues
Useful in distributed and complex systems

metadata.description: A system for enabling data exchange between components, processes, or services, either within the same machine or across a network.
pros: Supports scalable and modular system design
cons: Can introduce overhead depending on protocol

metadata.description: A communication pattern where data is exchanged between systems over a network using defined protocols and addressing.
A connection-oriented transport protocol that ensures reliable, ordered, and error-checked data delivery between systems.
cons: Higher latency due to connection overhead

metadata.description: A connectionless transport protocol that sends data without guaranteeing delivery, order, or duplication.
cons: Requires manual error handling if needed

metadata.description: An application-layer protocol built on top of TCP for structured request-response communication between clients and servers.
cons: Higher overhead due to headers and structure

metadata.description: A communication pattern that enables data exchange between processes within the same machine without using network protocols.
A unidirectional communication mechanism that allows one process to send data to another through a continuous byte stream.
A local inter-process communication mechanism using socket interfaces, providing bidirectional communication within the same machine.
pros: Faster than TCP for local communication

metadata.description: A memory architecture pattern where multiple components or agents access a common in-memory data store for fast data sharing and coordination. It is commonly used in concurrent systems, agent-based architectures, and high-performance applications to reduce communication overhead between services or threads.
pros: Simplifies data sharing in single-process architectures
cons: Risk of race conditions and concurrency issues
Hard to scale across distributed systems
Data consistency challenges without proper locking or synchronization

metadata.description: A system for storing and managing state and context, allowing the agent to maintain short-term session data and long-term knowledge across interactions.

Temporary memory used to store context during an active session or ongoing interaction. It holds recent information needed for immediate reasoning and decision-making, and is typically cleared or reset when the session ends.
pros: Reduces need for repeated computations or API calls
cons: Data is lost after session ends
Not suitable for long-term knowledge storage
Requires additional systems for persistence if needed

metadata.description: Persistent memory storage that retains information across sessions. It allows agents or systems to store, retrieve, and reuse knowledge over time, enabling continuity, personalization, and learning from past interactions.
pros: Can be queried anytime for historical context
cons: Potential latency compared to in-memory systems
Requires careful data management and indexing
Risk of storing outdated or irrelevant information if not maintained

metadata.description: In-memory storage for fast data access.
Caches data in short-term storage with a defined expiration time (TTL). Reduces repeated data fetching by serving cached results when available.
pros: Reduces repeated requests to source systems
cons: Risk of stale data if TTL is not well managed
Cache misses still require fetching from source

metadata.description: Singleton pattern ensures a single shared instance holding state in memory. It enables extremely fast data access and synchronization across the application.
cons: Only one shared instance (can become a bottleneck)
Requires careful state management to avoid conflicts
Data is lost when the process restarts
Harder to scale in distributed systems

metadata.description: Persistent storage on disk used for long-term data retention.
Stores data directly in files (e.g., JSON, text, binary) on disk without a database engine.
Stores and manages structured data using a database engine that provides querying, indexing, and transaction support.
cons: More complex to set up and maintain
Overhead compared to simple file storage

metadata.description: A database for storing vector embeddings and performing semantic similarity search, used in RAG, recommendation, and search systems.
pros: Enables semantic similarity search instead of exact matching
Scales well for large embedding datasets
cons: More complex infrastructure compared to traditional databases
Requires careful tuning for accuracy vs performance trade-offs
Not suitable for exact transactional queries

metadata.description: A programming and system design concept that enables multiple tasks to be managed and executed in overlapping time periods. It improves system responsiveness, throughput, and resource utilization by allowing tasks to progress independently, even if they are not executed simultaneously.
A coroutine-based model for non-blocking async execution, used mainly for I/O-bound tasks such as network and API calls.
pros: More readable and maintainable than callback-based approaches
Low overhead compared to thread-based concurrency
Efficient for handling high I/O concurrency workloads
cons: Not suitable for CPU-bound workloads without parallel execution
Can introduce complexity in debugging async flows
Requires proper handling of async context and error propagation

metadata.description: A concurrency model that uses multiple operating system threads to execute tasks in parallel. It is well-suited for CPU-bound workloads such as large-scale data processing, computation-heavy tasks, and parallel algorithms.
pros: Utilizes multiple CPU cores for parallel execution
Can significantly improve throughput for heavy computations
cons: Risk of race conditions and shared state corruption
Higher memory overhead per thread compared to async models
Requires careful thread management and synchronization mechanisms

metadata.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI architecture that enhances large language models by retrieving relevant information from external knowledge sources (such as vector databases or document stores) and injecting it into the generation process to produce more accurate and context-aware responses.

A representation technique that transforms text or other data into dense numerical vectors in a high-dimensional space, enabling machines to capture semantic meaning and measure similarity between inputs.

A preprocessing technique that splits large documents into smaller, semantically meaningful segments to fit within the context window of large language models and improve retrieval performance in RAG systems.
pros: Ensures compatibility with LLM context window limits
Improves retrieval precision by narrowing search scope
Enables more efficient indexing in vector databases
cons: Poor chunking strategy can break semantic continuity
May lead to loss of contextual information across segments
Requires careful tuning of chunk size and overlap

metadata.description: An interface for accessing large language models through external APIs (such as OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, and similar providers) to perform text generation, reasoning, and tool orchestration without requiring local model deployment.
pros: No need for local GPU or heavy infrastructure
Easy access to state-of-the-art models and updates
cons: Network latency can affect response time
Usage cost based on token consumption
Potential data privacy and compliance concerns

metadata.description: A capability that allows a large language model to interact with external functions or tools to perform real-world actions, such as fetching data, executing code, calling APIs, or triggering system operations, extending the model beyond pure text generation.

A mechanism that enables large language models to decide when to invoke external functions and generate structured arguments in a predefined schema, allowing reliable integration with APIs and system tools.
pros: Enables reliable integration with external APIs and tools
Reduces ambiguity compared to free-form text outputs
cons: Dependent on model support and provider implementation
Limited flexibility compared to free-form reasoning
Requires strict schema design and validation

metadata.description: A set of strategies and mechanisms for detecting, managing, and recovering from runtime errors in a system, ensuring graceful degradation, system stability, and preventing unexpected crashes during execution.
Rust's Result<T, E> and Option<T> types that encode errors and optional values directly into the type system, enforcing explicit handling of success, failure, and absence cases at compile time.
pros: Compile-time safety through the type system
Makes control flow and error handling explicit and predictable
cons: More verbose compared to exception-based error handling
Can increase boilerplate in complex error chains
Requires explicit handling of all cases

metadata.description: A fault-tolerance mechanism that automatically retries failed operations caused by transient errors, using exponential backoff to progressively increase delay between retries and prevent system overload.
pros: Improves resilience against transient network or service failures
Enables automatic recovery without manual intervention
Reduces impact of temporary infrastructure instability
cons: Increases overall latency during failure conditions
Can amplify load if misconfigured (retry storms)
Requires careful tuning of retry limits and backoff strategy

metadata.description: A process of converting in-memory data structures into a structured format that can be stored, transmitted over a network, or reconstructed later. It enables interoperability between systems and persistent data exchange.
A high-performance serialization framework used to convert data structures for applications, databases, scripts, and systems, with compile-time type safety via derive macros.
pros: Strong type safety at compile time
Supports multiple formats (JSON, YAML, TOML, etc.)
cons: Longer compile times due to macro expansion and code generation
Steeper learning curve for advanced customization

tech.description: General techniques or concepts applied across projects

pattern.description: Reusable design solution or technique to solve common problems in software architecture

stor.description: Technologies or systems responsible for storing, retrieving, and managing data

impl.description: Specific implementations or tools used to realize techniques
